# Inflection

[build status]

Inflection is a string transformation library.  It singularizes and pluralizes
English words, and transforms strings from CamelCase to underscored string.
Inflection is a port of [Ruby on Rails]' [inflector] to Python.

## Resources

- [Documentation](https://inflection.readthedocs.io/)
- [Issue Tracker](http://github.com/jpvanhal/inflection/issues)
- [Code](http://github.com/jpvanhal/inflection)
- [Development Version](http://github.com/jpvanhal/inflection/zipball/master#egg=Inflection-dev)

[build status]: http://travis-ci.org/jpvanhal/inflection
[inflector]: http://api.rubyonrails.org/classes/ActiveSupport/Inflector.html
[ruby on rails]: http://rubyonrails.org

modules.name: inflection
documentation:
inflection
~~~~~~~~~~~~

A port of Ruby on Rails' inflector to Python.

:copyright: (c) 2012-2020 by Janne Vanhala

:license: MIT, see LICENSE for more details.
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: inflection._irregular
documentation:
A convenience function to add appropriate rules to plurals and singular
for irregular words.

:param singular: irregular word in singular form
:param plural: irregular word in plural form

functions.name: inflection.camelize
documentation:
Convert strings to CamelCase.

Examples:

```
>>> camelize("device_type")
'DeviceType'
>>> camelize("device_type", False)
'deviceType'
```

{func}`camelize` can be thought of as a inverse of {func}`underscore`,
although there are some cases where that does not hold:

```
>>> camelize(underscore("IOError"))
'IoError'
```

```{eval-rst}

lowerCamelCase. Defaults to `True`.
```

functions.name: inflection.dasherize
documentation:
Replace underscores with dashes in the string.

Example:

```
>>> dasherize("puni_puni")
'puni-puni'
```

functions.name: inflection.humanize
documentation:
Capitalize the first word and turn underscores into spaces and strip a
trailing `"_id"`, if any. Like {func}`titleize`, this is meant for
creating pretty output.

Examples:

```
>>> humanize("employee_salary")
'Employee salary'
>>> humanize("author_id")
'Author'
```

functions.name: inflection.ordinal
documentation:
Return the suffix that should be added to a number to denote the position
in an ordered sequence such as 1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th.

Examples:

```
>>> ordinal(1)
'st'
>>> ordinal(2)
'nd'
>>> ordinal(1002)
'nd'
>>> ordinal(1003)
'rd'
>>> ordinal(-11)
'th'
>>> ordinal(-1021)
'st'
```

functions.name: inflection.ordinalize
documentation:
Turn a number into an ordinal string used to denote the position in an
ordered sequence such as 1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th.

Examples:

```
>>> ordinalize(1)
'1st'
>>> ordinalize(2)
'2nd'
>>> ordinalize(1002)
'1002nd'
>>> ordinalize(1003)
'1003rd'
>>> ordinalize(-11)
'-11th'
>>> ordinalize(-1021)
'-1021st'
```

functions.name: inflection.parameterize
documentation:
Replace special characters in a string so that it may be used as part of a
'pretty' URL.

Example:

```
>>> parameterize(u"Donald E. Knuth")
'donald-e-knuth'
```

functions.name: inflection.pluralize
documentation:
Return the plural form of a word.

Examples:

```
>>> pluralize("posts")
'posts'
>>> pluralize("octopus")
'octopi'
>>> pluralize("sheep")
'sheep'
>>> pluralize("CamelOctopus")
'CamelOctopi'
```

functions.name: inflection.singularize
documentation:
Return the singular form of a word, the reverse of {func}`pluralize`.

Examples:

```
>>> singularize("posts")
'post'
>>> singularize("octopi")
'octopus'
>>> singularize("sheep")
'sheep'
>>> singularize("word")
'word'
>>> singularize("CamelOctopi")
'CamelOctopus'
```

functions.name: inflection.tableize
documentation:
Create the name of a table like Rails does for models to table names. This
method uses the {func}`pluralize` method on the last word in the string.

Examples:

```
>>> tableize('RawScaledScorer')
'raw_scaled_scorers'
>>> tableize('egg_and_ham')
'egg_and_hams'
>>> tableize('fancyCategory')
'fancy_categories'
```

functions.name: inflection.titleize
documentation:
Capitalize all the words and replace some characters in the string to
create a nicer looking title. {func}`titleize` is meant for creating pretty
output.

Examples:

```
>>> titleize("man from the boondocks")
'Man From The Boondocks'
>>> titleize("x-men: the last stand")
'X Men: The Last Stand'
>>> titleize("TheManWithoutAPast")
'The Man Without A Past'
>>> titleize("raiders_of_the_lost_ark")
'Raiders Of The Lost Ark'
```

functions.name: inflection.transliterate
documentation:
Replace non-ASCII characters with an ASCII approximation. If no
approximation exists, the non-ASCII character is ignored. The string must
be `unicode`.

Examples:

```
>>> transliterate('älämölö')
'alamolo'
>>> transliterate('Ærøskøbing')
'rskbing'
```

functions.name: inflection.underscore
documentation:
Make an underscored, lowercase form from the expression in the string.

Example:

```
>>> underscore("DeviceType")
'device_type'
```

As a rule of thumb you can think of {func}`underscore` as the inverse of
{func}`camelize`, though there are cases where that does not hold:

```
>>> camelize(underscore("IOError"))
'IoError'
```
